steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The team's conclusion: differences in dogs' head shape (anatomical features) affect their ability to distinguish speech from nonspeech. A supporting finding must (1) compare head shapes and (2) involve a speech-vs.-nonspeech distinction.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "The pattern of brain activity that long-headed dogs showed when hearing the scrambled recording was different from the pattern of brain activity that short-headed dogs showed when hearing the language they were accustomed to.": compares two different conditions across two groups rather than showing how head shape affects the speech/nonspeech distinction — the comparison is muddled and doesn't isolate the relevant variable. ✗
  • "Compared with shorter-headed dogs, longer-headed dogs showed a greater difference in brain activity when hearing either Spanish or Hungarian than when hearing the scrambled recording.": directly links head shape to a larger speech-vs.-nonspeech gap in brain activity, exactly what the conclusion predicts. ✓
  • "Compared with shorter-headed dogs, longer-headed dogs showed a greater difference in brain activity when hearing the language they were accustomed to than when hearing the other language.": compares two speech stimuli (one language vs. another), not speech vs. nonspeech — doesn't address the conclusion. ✗
  • "Long-headed dogs accustomed to hearing Spanish tended to show more brain activity when hearing Spanish than long-headed dogs accustomed to hearing Hungarian showed when hearing Hungarian.": compares two groups of long-headed dogs hearing their respective languages — ignores head-shape comparison and the nonspeech condition entirely. ✗
That matches the correct answer — B is correct.
